votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification, using an iterative approach for the auxiliary function which is functionally equivalent to the Coq recursive definition.
The Python code correctly implements the bracket counting logic, matching the Coq recursive definition and specifications.
The Python code implements the same logic as the Coq code, using an iterative approach instead of recursion for correct_bracketing_aux, and correctly translates the pre and spec functions.

The Python code correctly implements the same bracket matching logic as the Coq code. The auxiliary function tracks depth, incrementing on '<', decrementing on '>' (returning False if depth is 0), and returns True only if depth is 0 at the end. The precondition checks all characters are '<' or '>'. The spec checks if the result matches correct_bracketing. All concrete behaviors match.

The Python code correctly implements the same bracket-matching logic as the Coq code. Both use a depth counter that increments on '<', decrements on '>', returns False if depth goes negative on '>', and returns True only if depth is 0 at the end. The pre-condition checks all characters are '<' or '>', and the spec checks if the result matches correct_bracketing. The iterative Python version produces the same results as the recursive Coq version on all concrete inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same bracket-matching logic as the Coq code. The auxiliary function tracks depth, incrementing on '<', decrementing on '>' (returning False if depth is 0), and returns True only if depth is 0 at the end. The precondition checks all characters are '<' or '>', and the spec checks if the result matches correct_bracketing. Both implementations produce identical results on all concrete inputs.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: it tracks a depth counter, returns false on premature '>', ignores non-bracket chars, and checks depth==0 at the end. The precondition and spec are implemented consistently.

Both Coq and Python increment depth on '<', decrement on '>' with early false if depth is zero, ignore any other characters, and finally return whether depth is zero. The precondition and spec are also mirrored exactly.

Both implementations track a depth counter, incrementing on '<', decrementing on '>' with failure if depth would go negative, and succeed iff final depth is 0. Other characters are ignored in both. The precondition and spec are also matched exactly.
